questions.question: In a PKI hierarchy, the root CA's primary role is to:
options: Anchor trust by signing intermediate CA certificates whose keys issue end-entity certificates
Issue TLS certificates directly for every internal microservice daily
Replace the need for certificate expiration dates
Store application passwords in encrypted form
questions.explanation: BSRS chapter 11 describes PKI as a chain of trust: closely guarded root CAs sign intermediates, which issue leaf certificates. Keeping roots offline limits blast radius if an issuing CA is compromised.

questions.question: Google's PKI lessons in chapter 11 emphasize automated certificate rotation because:
options: Short-lived certificates reduce the window of abuse if a key is compromised and avoid manual renewal outages
Certificates should never expire to prevent service interruption
Manual yearly renewal scales better than automation at large fleet size
Rotation eliminates the need for private key protection
questions.explanation: Chapter 11 documents moving from long-lived manual certs to automated issuance and frequent rotation — shrinking compromise impact and removing human error from renewal.

questions.question: If an intermediate CA private key is suspected compromised, chapter 11's response priority includes:
options: Revoke affected certificates, rotate the compromised CA, and re-issue downstream certs while preserving trust chain integrity
Wait for certificate natural expiration because revocation is unnecessary
Publish the private key publicly to prove transparency
Disable TLS entirely until the next quarterly maintenance window
questions.explanation: Chapter 11 CA lessons cover incident playbooks: rapid revocation, CA rotation, and fleet-wide re-issuance. PKI incidents are high-severity because trust anchor compromise affects all dependents.

questions.question: Certificate Transparency logs help detect mis-issued certificates by:
options: Providing publicly auditable records of issued certs so domain owners can detect unauthorized issuance
Encrypting private keys before storage in DNS
Replacing OCSP stapling with DNS TXT records only
Eliminating the need for CA hierarchy
questions.explanation: Chapter 11 discusses CT as a detective control: append-only public logs enable monitoring for certs issued without authorization — complementing preventive CA access controls.

questions.question: The primary defense against SQL injection in chapter 12's secure coding guidance is:
options: Parameterized queries or prepared statements that separate query structure from user-supplied data
Escaping user input by manually replacing single quotes only in production
Storing SQL queries exclusively in client-side JavaScript
Using string concatenation with input validation as the sole control
questions.explanation: Chapter 12 treats parameterization as the canonical SQLi defense: the database driver binds data separately from SQL structure, preventing attacker-controlled input from altering query semantics.

questions.question: An ORM query builder concatenates user input into a raw SQL fragment via string interpolation. Chapter 12 would classify this as:
options: Still vulnerable to SQL injection because ORM use does not automatically parameterize unsafe raw fragments
Safe because ORMs eliminate all injection classes by default
Safe if the ORM is open source
Safe because injection only affects NoSQL databases
questions.explanation: Chapter 12 warns that ORMs provide safe APIs only when used correctly. Raw SQL fragments with interpolated user data reintroduce injection regardless of the surrounding framework.

questions.question: To prevent stored XSS when rendering user comments in HTML, chapter 12 recommends:
options: Context-appropriate output encoding or sanitization before insertion into the DOM
Reflecting user input verbatim to preserve formatting fidelity
Relying on HTTPS alone to neutralize script injection
Disabling browser JavaScript globally for all users
questions.explanation: Chapter 12's XSS defenses center on encoding for the specific output context (HTML body, attribute, JavaScript). TLS protects transport, not stored script execution in browsers.

questions.question: Content Security Policy (CSP) serves as a defense-in-depth measure against XSS by:
options: Restricting which script sources may execute, limiting damage when an injection point exists
Replacing the need for input validation and output encoding entirely
Encrypting HTML responses so browsers cannot parse tags
Blocking all HTTP requests including same-origin API calls unconditionally
questions.explanation: Chapter 12 positions CSP as layered defense: even if encoding fails, a strict CSP can block inline script execution. It complements — not replaces — secure coding practices.

questions.question: Fuzz testing in chapter 13 differs from unit testing primarily because fuzzing:
options: Generates malformed or unexpected inputs automatically to explore code paths humans rarely anticipate
Requires manual writing of every input case before execution
Only validates happy-path API contracts
Replaces the need for code review
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 describes fuzzing as automated adversarial input generation — finding memory corruption, panics, and logic bugs in parsers and protocol handlers that manual tests miss.

questions.question: Continuous fuzzing integrated into the build pipeline primarily provides:
options: Ongoing discovery of regressions and new vulnerabilities as code changes, not just one-time pre-release testing
Guaranteed proof that no bugs exist in the codebase
Elimination of static analysis tooling
Replacement for all manual QA user acceptance testing
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 advocates continuous fuzzing as code evolves — new parsers and refactors introduce fresh attack surface. Fuzzing complements static analysis; neither proves absence of all bugs.

questions.question: Static analysis in CI catches vulnerabilities by:
options: Examining source or bytecode without execution to flag dangerous patterns such as injection sinks and unsafe API usage
Running production traffic against staging environments
Measuring runtime CPU utilization of security checks
Replacing dependency version pinning
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 covers SAST as pre-deployment analysis: taint tracking, pattern matchers, and type-aware checkers find bug classes before code ships — complementary to dynamic fuzzing.

questions.question: High false-positive rates in static analysis tooling most undermine security when teams:
options: Ignore all findings including true positives due to alert fatigue — chapter 13 recommends tuning, suppression with justification, and prioritization
Fix every warning without triage regardless of exploitability
Disable the compiler to reduce warnings
Run static analysis only once at initial project creation
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 acknowledges SAST limitations. Sustainable programs tune rules, track suppressions, and prioritize reachable vulnerabilities — otherwise tooling becomes noise and real bugs slip through.

questions.question: Binary provenance in chapter 14 records:
options: Cryptographically verifiable metadata linking an artifact to its exact source revision, build configuration, and builder identity
Only the file size and MIME type of the deployed binary
The marketing version name without commit hash
User passwords used during the build process
questions.explanation: Chapter 14's provenance model enables verifying that a deployed binary was built from expected source in a trusted environment — foundational for supply chain security.

questions.question: Verifiable builds require hermetic build environments because:
options: Non-hermetic builds may incorporate undeclared inputs, making reproducibility and provenance claims unverifiable
Hermetic builds run faster by skipping dependency downloads
Reproducibility is impossible for compiled languages
Hermetic builds eliminate the need for code signing
questions.explanation: Chapter 14 ties hermeticity to verifiability: only declared inputs participate in the build. Undeclared ambient state breaks reproducibility and allows supply chain tampering to go undetected.

questions.question: A deployment policy requiring verified provenance before promotion prevents:
options: Running binaries whose origin cannot be traced to an authorized build pipeline and source commit
All rollbacks to previous known-good versions
Use of any container orchestration platform
Automated canary analysis during rollout
questions.explanation: Chapter 14 describes policy enforcement at deploy time: reject artifacts lacking valid provenance signatures — blocking tampered or manually built binaries from production.

questions.question: Reproducible builds support provenance verification when:
options: Independent rebuild from the same source and config produces bit-identical or semantically equivalent artifacts, confirming the published binary matches source
Build timestamps match exactly across rebuilds
Developers build locally on laptops and upload binaries directly
Only dynamic languages can achieve reproducibility
questions.explanation: Chapter 14 explains reproducibility as the ability to recreate artifacts: matching outputs confirm provenance claims weren't forged and detect build-time injection.

questions.question: Immutable logging in chapter 15 means:
options: Log entries cannot be altered or deleted after append without detection, preserving evidentiary integrity
Logs are stored only in volatile memory for performance
Engineers may freely edit production logs to remove sensitive data post-incident
Log volume is capped at zero to prevent storage costs
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 defines immutability for audit and security logs: append-only storage with integrity protections (hash chains, WORM storage) so tampering is detectable.

questions.question: Hash-chained log entries primarily provide:
options: Tamper evidence: modifying a historical entry breaks subsequent hash links, revealing alteration
Compression of log payloads for long-term archival
Automatic encryption of all application secrets in logs
Elimination of the need for access controls on log readers
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 describes hash chaining as a lightweight integrity mechanism: each entry includes the prior hash, forming a detectable chain if an attacker edits history.

questions.question: Chapter 15 recommends separating security audit logs from application debug logs because:
options: Different retention, access controls, and immutability guarantees apply — debug logs are mutable and verbose; audit logs require strict integrity
Debug logs are more trustworthy than audit logs for compliance
Audit logs should be writable by all application service accounts
Combining logs reduces storage cost with no security trade-off
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 distinguishes log tiers: audit trails need restricted write access, long retention, and immutability; debug logs prioritize developer velocity and may be rotated or sampled.

questions.question: During a suspected breach, immutable audit logs support investigation because:
options: Investigators can trust the timeline was not retroactively altered by the attacker to cover tracks
Logs automatically identify the attacker without analysis
Immutable logs eliminate the need for network forensics
Attackers cannot read logs if they are immutable
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 positions immutable logging as forensic integrity: post-compromise, attackers often attempt log tampering. Append-only, tamper-evident storage preserves trustworthy evidence for IR and legal review.
